title: New Nonvolatile Memory Winners Emerge
gist: MRAM and RRAM are emerging as the winning embedded nonvolatile memory technologies for advanced (finFET and beyond) process nodes as embedded flash hits scaling limits at 28nm, while PCRAM is being left behind, FeRAM is getting a second wind via HZO-based scaling to 22nm, and a startup called Quinas is developing a novel III-V floating-gate 'UltraRAM' bit cell that targets DRAM-like performance with near-infinite retention.

themes.theme: Flash supply constraints driving NVM diversification

insights.claim: Everspin's Sean Dougherty says HBM, DRAM, and NAND flash suppliers are all straining to meet insatiable demand over the next couple of years, with major memory makers committing available volumes to a small number of large customers and decommitting from or exiting other industries entirely.
insights.speaker: Bryon Moyer
insights.so_what: Tightening flash/DRAM/HBM allocation toward large AI customers is directly pushing smaller/non-AI customers toward alternative nonvolatile memory technologies (MRAM, RRAM), a structural demand tailwind for the emerging-NVM sector independent of those technologies' own merits.
insights.quote: HBM, DRAM, and NAND are all pushing to satisfy what feels like an insatiable amount of demand over the next couple of years. The big guys are literally committing their available volumes to a small number of players and decommitting from or exiting other industries.
insights.locator: opening quote from Sean Dougherty, Everspin

themes.theme: MRAM and RRAM emerge as embedded-NVM winners, PCRAM left behind

insights.claim: MRAM and RRAM are likely to coexist rather than one displacing the other: RRAM offers lower cost while MRAM offers faster performance and better handling of extreme conditions; both outperform embedded flash on modern process nodes but neither threatens standalone (NAND) flash given NAND's fundamental cost advantage.
insights.speaker: Bryon Moyer
insights.so_what: This is a clear market-structure call: MRAM and RRAM will split the embedded-NVM market by use case rather than one winning outright, and neither is a threat to standalone flash economics — informs how investors should size each technology's addressable market (embedded only, not standalone).
insights.locator: summary paragraph after Key Takeaways

insights.claim: Despite years of development, PCRAM has no commercial standalone offerings and no apparent development efforts to port it to finFET nodes and beyond, according to both Synopsys and Infineon, effectively removing it from the competitive set for next-generation embedded NVM.
insights.speaker: Bryon Moyer
insights.so_what: PCRAM appears to be a dead-end technology for advanced nodes; investors or IP licensors with PCRAM exposure should treat it as a legacy/declining technology rather than a growth bet, confirmed independently by both an EDA memory-compiler vendor and a leading MCU maker.
insights.quote: There’s not been enough demand from any customer that says, ‘I want to be able to embed it, I want to be able to design it into my chip, and I need a compiler to do that.’
insights.locator: Daryl Seitzer (Synopsys) quote on PCRAM demand

insights.claim: Infineon's Robert Wiesner confirms the company sees no activity to bring PCRAM to finFET nodes, while active development is underway for both RRAM and MRAM, corroborating that PCRAM has effectively lost the race for next-generation embedded memory.
insights.speaker: Bryon Moyer
insights.so_what: Cross-corroboration from a major MCU/automotive chipmaker (Infineon) reinforces PCRAM's exit from the competitive roadmap, strengthening the case that RRAM/MRAM will be the default embedded NVM choices going forward.
insights.quote: PCRAM is on the market in planar CMOS technologies. However, we don’t observe any activities to bring PCRAM to finFET nodes, while there are development activities for RRAM and MRAM.
insights.locator: Robert Wiesner (Infineon) quote

insights.claim: Flash is fundamentally stuck at the 28nm node for embedded integration because process complexity for integrating flash into state-of-the-art CMOS became a significant concern at 28nm and beyond (e.g., finFET), while further shrink potential is stagnating — this is the core structural reason MRAM and RRAM are gaining share in embedded applications on advanced nodes.
insights.speaker: Bryon Moyer
insights.so_what: The 28nm embedded-flash wall is the single structural driver of the entire MRAM/RRAM growth narrative in this article — any MCU or SoC design that needs a finFET process node is mechanically forced toward MRAM or RRAM for code storage, a durable multi-year demand driver.
insights.locator: section 'Scaling as an important factor'

insights.claim: Foundries including Samsung, TSMC, and GlobalFoundries now offer both embedded MRAM and embedded RRAM, and customers are reportedly choosing RRAM for cost-sensitive commercial applications while choosing MRAM where performance and reliability matter most, particularly in industrial and automotive segments.
insights.speaker: Bryon Moyer
insights.so_what: All three major logic foundries now offer both technologies, confirming MRAM/RRAM are mainstream production options, not experimental; automotive and industrial customers concentrating on MRAM is a specific end-market signal for MRAM IP/foundry revenue.
insights.quote: Customers are picking RRAM whenever they have a commercial application, and they’re picking MRAM where they care about performance and reliability, which is industrial and automotive.
insights.locator: Sanjeev Aggarwal (Everspin CEO) quote

insights.claim: TSMC's technology roadmap for embedded RRAM/MRAM-class memory extends down to 12nm and 6nm nodes, according to Synopsys, indicating these NVM technologies are planned for multiple future process generations rather than a single node.
insights.speaker: Bryon Moyer
insights.so_what: A multi-generation foundry roadmap commitment (12nm and 6nm) de-risks the MRAM/RRAM technology thesis for investors, since it signals sustained foundry capex and IP investment rather than a one-off node offering.
insights.locator: Daryl Seitzer (Synopsys) quote on TSMC roadmap

insights.claim: MRAM read speeds are approaching roughly 10 picosecond read access, close to but not matching SRAM's picosecond-level speeds, according to GlobalFoundries, positioning MRAM as a plausible last-level-cache candidate that could partially address SRAM's stalled density scaling and outsized die-size impact.
insights.speaker: Bryon Moyer
insights.so_what: MRAM as a partial SRAM replacement for last-level cache is a specific, quantified technical claim (10 ps vs SRAM's sub-ps) relevant to chip architects and IP vendors evaluating on-die memory hierarchy alternatives amid the well-documented SRAM scaling wall.
insights.quote: MRAM read speeds aren’t quite as fast as SRAM. They’re not close to picosecond read speeds, but they’re close to 10 picosecond read access.
insights.locator: Jamie Schaeffer (GlobalFoundries) quote

insights.claim: MRAM has three technology generations: toggle MRAM (oldest, commercialized standalone by Everspin), spin-transfer torque (STT, current commercial generation with a smaller bit cell), and spin-orbit transfer (SOT, still in research with no commercial offering and likely years from commercialization).
insights.speaker: Bryon Moyer
insights.so_what: SOT-MRAM remains a multi-year-out technology despite promising performance/endurance tradeoffs, meaning investors should not expect near-term commercial SOT products; STT is the current investable commercial MRAM generation.
insights.locator: section 'MRAM has the speed advantage'

insights.claim: Everspin's Sean Dougherty predicts MRAM will play a significant role in edge AI because density and read/write performance matter more in edge AI embedded systems than in other application classes, positioning MRAM as a beneficiary of the broader edge-AI silicon buildout.
insights.speaker: Bryon Moyer
insights.so_what: MRAM vendors are explicitly targeting edge AI as a growth market, giving investors a concrete end-market thesis (beyond automotive/aerospace/industrial) to watch for MRAM revenue growth as edge-AI device shipments scale.
insights.quote: We believe that MRAM is going to play a significant contribution in edge AI. The density and the read and write performance are much more important on those edge AI embedded systems.
insights.locator: Sean Dougherty quote on MRAM and edge AI

insights.claim: There is industry disagreement over MRAM's vulnerability to external magnetic fields: Infineon's Robert Wiesner says magnetic susceptibility remains a serious concern for some applications with magnetic field specs applying both in production and in the field, while Everspin's Sanjeev Aggarwal maintains their STT MRAM is magnetically immune by the nature of its materials.
insights.speaker: Bryon Moyer
insights.so_what: An unresolved technical dispute between a major MCU maker (Infineon) and the leading standalone MRAM vendor (Everspin) over magnetic-field robustness is a live risk factor for MRAM adoption in magnetically hostile environments — worth monitoring for independent third-party validation before treating either claim as settled.
insights.quote: Our STT MRAM is magnetically immune just by the nature of the materials that we’re using.
insights.locator: Sanjeev Aggarwal (Everspin) quote rebutting magnetic-field concerns

themes.theme: RRAM technical characteristics and Infineon's production data

insights.claim: RRAM comes in two flavors — conductive-bridge RAM (CBRAM), which forms and destroys a metal filament, and OxRAM, which moves oxygen vacancies — and OxRAM is winning the competition because its metal-oxide materials and inert electrodes translate into cleaner back-end CMOS integration and lower contamination risk than CBRAM's electrochemical metal-ion movement.
insights.speaker: Bryon Moyer
insights.so_what: OxRAM's emergence as the dominant RRAM sub-technology (over CBRAM) is a specific technical bet investors evaluating RRAM IP vendors or foundry offerings should confirm alignment with, since the two variants have materially different contamination and reliability risk profiles.
insights.locator: section 'RRAM is cheaper'

insights.claim: RRAM's main advantage is process simplicity — a small capacitor (bottom electrode, dielectric, top electrode) in the BEOL stack plus a standard CMOS select device, requiring no exotic materials or special tools, according to Infineon's Robert Wiesner.
insights.speaker: Bryon Moyer
insights.so_what: RRAM's low integration complexity (no exotic materials/tools) supports a lower-cost manufacturing thesis versus MRAM, reinforcing why RRAM is favored for cost-sensitive commercial/consumer applications per Everspin's earlier comment.
insights.quote: The main advantage I see is the simplicity of the RRAM technology process — a small capacitor (bottom electrode, dielectric, top electrode) in the BEOL stack, plus a select device in standard CMOS, no exotic materials, no special tools needed.
insights.locator: Robert Wiesner quote on RRAM simplicity

insights.claim: Infineon's ATV RRAM, developed with TSMC and selected for MCUs beyond the 28nm node, demonstrated data retention of over 1,000 hours at 175°C (equivalent to roughly eight years at 125°C, or 200,000 years at 40°C), 250,000 code-change endurance, 15.2-nanosecond read times, and an extended -40°C to 160°C operating range.
insights.speaker: Bryon Moyer
insights.so_what: This is a concrete, quantified production-grade RRAM performance dataset from a real Infineon-TSMC co-development, giving investors hard benchmarks (retention, endurance, speed, temperature range) to compare against competing embedded-NVM offerings from other foundries.
insights.locator: paragraph on Infineon's ATV RRAM specs

insights.claim: Infineon found RRAM easier to build than MRAM for its MCU applications, but the company is also separately evaluating MRAM as a potential replacement for SRAM in data-storage roles — indicating Infineon is pursuing both technologies for different purposes within its own roadmap rather than picking one winner.
insights.speaker: Bryon Moyer
insights.so_what: A single major chipmaker (Infineon) pursuing both RRAM (easier, for code storage) and MRAM (for SRAM replacement) simultaneously validates the article's core coexistence thesis with a real-world example, rather than treating it as speculative analyst framing.
insights.locator: paragraph ending 'One that's quietly shipping' section lead-in

insights.claim: FeRAM offers very high write endurance and much lower write power than resistive memories — CEA-Leti's Laurent Grenouillet notes ferroelectric memories only need voltage applied across the capacitor with no appreciable current, while resistive memories require passing current through the device, consuming typically 100x more energy.
insights.speaker: Bryon Moyer
insights.so_what: A quantified 100x write-energy advantage over resistive memories is a strong differentiator for FeRAM in power-constrained, write-intensive applications like datalogging — a concrete number investors can use to evaluate FeRAM's competitive positioning against RRAM/MRAM in specific niches.
insights.quote: The beauty of ferroelectric memories is that you only need to apply voltage across the FeCap [with no appreciable current] to write the information. With resistive memories you need to pass current through the device, and this consumes typically 100× more energy.
insights.locator: Laurent Grenouillet quote on FeRAM write power

insights.claim: FeRAM has been commercialized for years using PZT dielectric but was limited to 130nm because PZT is not CMOS-compatible and cannot be deposited via atomic layer deposition; CEA-Leti has now successfully implemented FeRAM at 22nm using hafnium zirconium oxide (HZO), a CMOS-fab-compatible, ALD-depositable material, and is working to scale a 7nm-thick film down to 5 or 4nm.
insights.speaker: Bryon Moyer
insights.so_what: The PZT-to-HZO materials switch is the key unlock enabling FeRAM's jump from a 130nm scaling ceiling to 22nm (and a path toward 4-5nm), a concrete technical breakthrough that could reopen FeRAM as a competitive embedded-NVM and potentially DRAM-adjacent technology after years of stagnation.
insights.locator: paragraph on CEA-Leti's 22nm HZO FeRAM

insights.claim: Embedded FeRAM requires only two to four additional masks for integration, and at 20nm it can be 2.5x smaller than SRAM, according to CEA-Leti's Laurent Grenouillet, giving it both a low integration-cost profile and a meaningful density advantage over SRAM at that node.
insights.speaker: Bryon Moyer
insights.so_what: A quantified 2.5x density advantage over SRAM at 20nm gives FeRAM a specific, comparable metric versus MRAM's cache-replacement ambitions — relevant for chip architects and IP licensors comparing SRAM-alternative memory options.
insights.quote: Embedded FeRAM needs between two and four additional masks for integration. At 20 nm, it can be 2.5× smaller than SRAM.
insights.locator: Laurent Grenouillet quote on FeRAM mask count and density

insights.claim: Micron built a 32-Gb FeRAM it called NVDRAM as a nonvolatile DRAM alternative a couple of years prior to this article, but has not commercialized it, and it remains unclear whether the project was shelved due to a technical issue or simply deprioritized in resource allocation.
insights.speaker: Bryon Moyer
insights.so_what: Micron's uncommercialized 32-Gb NVDRAM project is a data point suggesting even a major memory maker found the DRAM-replacement FeRAM opportunity technically or commercially unready — a cautionary signal for how difficult displacing DRAM with any NVM technology remains, including for Quinas's UltraRAM ambitions.
insights.locator: paragraph on Micron's NVDRAM project

insights.claim: Startup Quinas has developed 'UltraRAM,' a compound-semiconductor floating-gate memory device using quantum resonant tunneling through engineered III-V heterostructures (three AlSb barriers separating two InAs quantum wells) instead of conventional dielectric-oxide tunneling used in flash, according to CEO James Ashford-Pook and CTO Peter Hodgson.
insights.speaker: Bryon Moyer
insights.so_what: UltraRAM represents a genuinely novel bit-cell physics approach (not an incremental variant of existing NVM), making Quinas a distinct, differentiated bet for investors seeking exposure to non-consensus memory technology outside the MRAM/RRAM/FeRAM mainstream.
insights.quote: UltraRAM is a compound-semiconductor floating-gate memory device — effectively a III‑V analog of flash memory — but with a fundamentally different charge transfer mechanism. Instead of tunneling through a dielectric oxide, it uses quantum resonant tunneling through engineered III‑V heterostructures.
insights.locator: James Ashford-Pook quote on UltraRAM mechanism

insights.claim: Quinas claims UltraRAM shows no degradation after 10 million endurance cycles and extrapolates data retention to at least 10,000 years based on a conservative straight-line estimate, though CTO Peter Hodgson says the true retention may be effectively infinite and the company deliberately understated the claim to avoid controversy.
insights.speaker: Bryon Moyer
insights.so_what: A 10,000-year retention claim (even if conservative) combined with 10M-cycle endurance would be an extreme outlier versus flash/MRAM/RRAM/FeRAM if validated at production scale — a claim investors should treat as an early, unverified lab result requiring independent third-party confirmation before underwriting.
insights.quote: We’ve got infinite retention time. We didn’t want to say that in the paper because that may be a bit controversial. So instead, we drew a straight line, which is probably underestimating our retention, but it still goes to 10,000 years, which is more than enough for any memory.
insights.locator: Peter Hodgson quote on retention estimate

insights.claim: Because UltraRAM's III-V bit-cell materials (GaSb, InAs, AlSb — the '6.1 angstrom family') are considered contaminants in silicon fabs, Quinas must manufacture the memory in a III-V fab rather than a standard CMOS facility, and is evaluating either bonding a separate silicon control chip to the III-V memory die or building control logic directly on a III-V wafer.
insights.speaker: Bryon Moyer
insights.so_what: The requirement for a dedicated III-V fab (rather than standard CMOS) is a structural manufacturing constraint that could cap UltraRAM's near-term volume regardless of technical merit, since III-V foundry capacity is far smaller than CMOS capacity — a key scaling risk investors must weigh against the technology's performance claims.
insights.locator: paragraph on III-V fab requirements

insights.claim: Quinas projects that at a scaled-down 20nm production node, UltraRAM could achieve 1-nanosecond switching speed — competitive with, and possibly faster than, DRAM — while requiring no refresh cycle (unlike DRAM), consuming less power, and using a 6F² cell size (with a longer-term path to 4F²) that is cost-competitive with DRAM.
insights.speaker: Bryon Moyer
insights.so_what: If validated at scale, a no-refresh, DRAM-speed-competitive, lower-power nonvolatile memory would be a category-redefining technology (a genuine DRAM disruptor); however this is a projection from unscaled test structures, not measured production silicon, so investors should treat it as high-risk/high-reward and multi-year out (Quinas targets market entry in 2029).
insights.quote: If we scale down the devices, we predict that this will correspond to a 1-ns switching speed at 20nm, which is competitive with DRAM — and in fact may be a little bit faster than DRAM.
insights.locator: Peter Hodgson quote on projected 20nm performance

insights.claim: Quinas is deliberately targeting low-volume, high-value initial markets — where energy efficiency, endurance, and persistence matter more than cost per bit — rather than volume markets first, and is pivoting toward neuromorphic/in-memory computing applications given the bit cell's potential to handle multiple charge levels, while expecting to reach market in 2029.
insights.speaker: Bryon Moyer
insights.so_what: A 2029 market-entry target with an initial niche (not mass-market) go-to-market strategy signals Quinas is a long-dated, early-stage bet; the neuromorphic/in-memory computing pivot also broadens its addressable market beyond simple NVM replacement, worth tracking for follow-on funding rounds as validation milestones.
insights.locator: final section 'Performance that targets DRAM'
